mechanism: Exposure to repeated identical product instances increases perceptual homogeneity and functional unity around shared benefits. These perceptual properties activate concepts of product entitativity (unified groupness), which cascades to increased confidence in group member capabilities and generalized to per-unit efficacy judgments. Mental imagery generation and group perception processes mediate the effect.

Cognitive salience of goal conflict activates heightened need-to-justify heuristic. This psychological process persists to subsequent unrelated decisions, biasing time allocation toward options offering more concrete, justifiable benefits (work) versus experiential benefits (leisure). Processing fluency and accessibility of salient justifications mediate the effect.
domain: consumer behavior, goal conflict, time allocation

mechanism: Financial constraint salience increases mental focus on future benefit streams and longevity considerations. When evaluating discretionary purchases, constrained consumers weight the durability and persistence of consumption utility more heavily. This longevity concern shifts preference toward physically enduring goods over ephemeral experiential benefits.

Economic scarcity shifts reproductive value assessment favoring offspring with higher certainty of reproductive success. Parental resource allocation optimizes expected genetic propagation conditional on environmental resource availability. Reproductive variance between sexes and mating market asymmetries create conditional fitness trade-offs in offspring investment.
domain: evolutionary psychology, parental spending, economic behavior

mechanism: Circular versus angular geometric properties activate softness and hardness concepts, making these attributes mentally accessible. Mental imagery generation in visuospatial working memory integrates activated concepts with product information. Imagery-generated attributes influence product evaluations through semantic congruence between visual and verbal cues.
domain: visual marketing, brand design, cognitive psychology

mechanism: Lateral display positions of items interact with consumers' natural left-to-right spatial magnitude representation. Congruent arrangements (healthy-left, unhealthy-right) enhance processing fluency and reduce decision difficulty. Increased processing ease strengthens self-control engagement and bias toward self-disciplined choices.
domain: sensory marketing, visual display, behavioral decision-making
